spec_ref: TDD-005 BFT consensus · tdd005/crates/tdd005_orchestrator/src/bft.rs (PBFT, equivocation log, view change)
description: Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance with n=4 replicas, f=1 byzantine fault tolerance, 2f+1=3-vote quorum, Ed25519-signed messages, and equivocation detection. Five scenarios: clean quorum, dissent, equivocation caught, quorum stalled, view change. Real Ed25519 signatures, real SHA3-256 action hashes.

phases.name: View change
phases.plain: replicas vote to advance view on timeout

records.summary: Leader (r0) proposes. r1/r2/r3 each verify and prepare. With 3 prepares (= quorum), every replica commits. The action runs.
records.aftermath: The committed action is appended to the chain, the per-instance state machine moves to Executed, and the orchestrator runs the tool. Any replica can prove the commit happened with quorum by presenting the 3 signed Commit messages.

beats.copy: Clean quorum — all 4 replicas agree. The leader proposes; three replicas verify and
agree. Two rounds of voting, no faults, no surprises. This is what PBFT looks like
when nothing's wrong.
PrePrepare phase. Leader for view 0 is r0. r0 signs the proposal (action + SHA3-256
hash) with its Ed25519 key and broadcasts to the other three replicas. The proposal
hash is what gets signed — not the raw action bytes.
Prepare phase. r1, r2, r3 each independently verify the proposal hash matches the
action bytes they received, sign their Prepares with their own Ed25519 keys, and
broadcast. 4 prepares (leader's implicit + r1/r2/r3) — quorum (3) reached.
Commit phase. All 4 replicas see the prepare quorum, each signs and broadcasts a
Commit. 4 Commits = quorum + 1. Every replica's state machine moves to Executed.
Verdict: COMMITTED. 4 prepares, 4 commits, no faults. The orchestrator runs the
tool. Any replica can prove the commit with 3+ signed Commit messages -- the proof
is self-contained, no audit service in the loop.

records.plain: One replica goes silent — quorum still reached
records.summary: Leader (r0) proposes a high-risk deploy. r1 and r2 prepare normally; r3 doesn't respond within the view timeout. r0 + r1 + r2 = 3 prepares = quorum. Commits without r3.
records.aftermath: r3's silence is recorded in the audit log (timeout, not signed refusal — in PBFT, an objecting replica simply doesn't respond; it doesn't sign a counter-vote). The action commits. r3 stays in the replica set; sustained timeouts may eventually drop it via operator policy.

beats.copy: One replica goes silent — quorum still reached. PBFT tolerates f=1 byzantine fault
out of n=4 replicas. One silent replica doesn't block consensus: the other three
still hit the 2f+1=3 quorum threshold. The system commits without it. That's what
'tolerates 1 fault' means.
PrePrepare phase. Leader r0 signs the proposal. This is a deploy-production call —
the stakes are real. r0 doesn't get to make this call alone; the next two rounds
collect votes from the other three replicas.
Prepare phase. r1 and r2 verify and Prepare. r3 doesn't respond at all — silent
through the entire view timeout. In PBFT a non-ratifying replica simply doesn't send
anything; no signed counter-vote (that would trigger the equivocation detector). r0
+ r1 + r2 = 3 prepares — quorum reached without r3.
Commit phase. r0, r1, r2 see the 3-prepare quorum and Commit. r3 stays silent
through this phase too. 3 Commits = quorum. The action runs.
Verdict: COMMITTED without r3. 3 of 4 replicas reached the 2f+1=3 quorum. r3's
silence is logged but doesn't get veto power -- a single non-responder can't block
consensus, by design.

r2 verified the proposal hashes match the action, signed and broadcast Prepare

r0 sees the prepare quorum, signs and broadcasts Commit
r1 sees the prepare quorum, signs and broadcasts Commit
r2 sees the prepare quorum, signs and broadcasts Commit
r3 stayed silent through the commit phase too — quorum already met without it

records.plain: Equivocation caught — byzantine replica detected, system still commits
records.summary: r0 proposes. r2 sends conflicting Prepare messages — one with action_hash A, another with action_hash B for the same sequence. r2 is flagged byzantine; both its votes are discarded. r0 + r1 + r3 = 3 honest prepares = quorum. The action commits anyway. PBFT survives the fault.
records.aftermath: r2 is marked byzantine in the equivocation log. Its signature key gets the byzantine flag — operator policy decides whether to evict. The action commits cleanly with 3 honest replicas; the byzantine replica's attempt to corrupt the vote is preserved as evidence in the audit trail.

beats.copy: Equivocation caught — byzantine replica detected, system still commits. A byzantine
replica votes on two different action hashes for the same sequence. PBFT tracks
(replica_id, sequence) → set-of-action-hashes. The moment a replica's set has >1
hash, it's flagged byzantine and BOTH of its votes are discarded. The protocol
survives because f=1: up to 1 byzantine fault is tolerated.
PrePrepare phase. Leader r0 signs the proposal cleanly. The (replica_id, sequence) →
set-of-hashes equivocation log starts tracking each replica's votes as they arrive.
Watch r2.
Prepare phase. r1 and r3 verify the proposal hash, sign, and broadcast. r2 sends TWO
Prepares with different action_hashes for the same sequence. The equivocation log
catches it on the second one. r2 is flagged byzantine; both its votes are discarded.
r0 (implicit) + r1 + r3 = 3 honest prepares — quorum reached.
Commit phase. r2's votes are excluded. Prepare quorum (3) was reached via r0 + r1 +
r3. Each of those three signs and broadcasts a Commit. 3 Commits = quorum. The
action commits cleanly — the byzantine replica didn't stop honest consensus.
Verdict: COMMITTED, with r2 flagged byzantine. The equivocation is preserved in the
audit log. r2's signature key gets the byzantine mark for operator review. This is
what 'tolerates 1 byzantine fault' means at runtime: correct answer reached even
when one of four replicas actively corrupts. n=3f+1 with f=1 was designed for
precisely this case.

records.plain: Quorum not reached — too many replicas offline
records.summary: r0 proposes. r1 prepares. r2 and r3 time out (network partition or process crash). Leader has its own prepare + 1 more = 2 votes. Quorum (3) not reached. Action does not commit.
records.aftermath: The pending consensus instance is held open until either the missing replicas come back or the view timeout fires. No silent rollback — the orchestrator sees "action not committed, quorum not reached" and can decide whether to retry, escalate, or fail loud.

beats.copy: Quorum not reached — too many replicas offline. PBFT needs 2f+1=3 honest replicas
voting yes. If two of the four are offline or fail to respond within 5000ms, the
leader sees only itself + maybe one prepare. 2 votes != quorum. The action doesn't
commit. Honest failure surfaces; no silent rollforward.
PrePrepare phase. Leader r0 signs and broadcasts as normal. The leader doesn't know
yet that two replicas won't respond -- that only surfaces when the view timer
expires.
Prepare phase. r1 verifies and Prepares. r2 and r3 don't respond within the 5000ms
view timeout. Leader + r1 = 2 prepares. Quorum (3) NOT reached.
Commit phase never starts. With only 2 prepares, the prepare quorum threshold wasn't
reached. Replicas don't progress to Commit. The consensus instance is held open.
Verdict: NOT COMMITTED. With 2 replicas offline, the system honestly cannot reach
quorum. The pending instance waits — no silent rollback, no
quietly-committed-with-fewer-votes. The orchestrator sees 'stalled' and can retry,
escalate, or fail loud.

r2 did not respond within the view timeout (5000ms)

records.plain: Leader times out — view change elects new leader
records.summary: r0 proposes then goes silent. After 5s view timeout, r1/r2/r3 send ViewChange messages. With 3 votes (quorum), view advances. r1 becomes new leader for view 1, re-proposes, consensus completes under r1.
records.aftermath: The view-change proof (signed ViewChange messages from quorum) is retained — anyone reviewing the audit can see the chain of custody for leadership. The original leader (r0) is marked stale; its reputation score drops in the orchestrator's leader-selection heuristic.

beats.copy: Leader times out — view change elects new leader. The leader (r0) goes silent for 5
seconds (timeout). Replicas vote to change views. Quorum (3) vote yes — view
advances 0→1. New leader = replicas[1 % 4] = r1. r1 re-proposes the action under the
new view; consensus completes. Liveness preserved despite leader failure.
PrePrepare phase. r0 is leader for view 0 (replicas[view % 4]). r0 has gone silent
for 5+ seconds. No PrePrepare signed, nothing broadcast. The view timer is ticking;
replicas will soon vote for a new leader.
Prepare phase never starts under view 0. The leader's PrePrepare never arrived —
nothing to verify against. Replicas pivot to voting on a new view instead.
Commit phase never starts under view 0. Replicas observe the leader timeout and
broadcast ViewChange messages.
Verdict: COMMITTED under view 1 after view change. The view-change proof (3 signed
ViewChange messages) is retained. Anyone reviewing the audit can see leadership
transferred r0 → r1 cleanly, with cryptographic evidence at every step.

r2 verifies the re-proposal under view 1, signs and broadcasts Prepare
r3 verifies the re-proposal under view 1, signs and broadcasts Prepare

r3 sees the new-view prepare quorum, signs and broadcasts Commit
